title: AI Fundamentals
description: Core AI, ML, and GenAI concepts every candidate should be able to explain clearly.

questions.title: Explain the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI.
questions.scenario: A business stakeholder asks whether every AI roadmap item should use an LLM.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer separates the broad field of AI from machine learning systems that learn patterns from data, deep learning models based on neural networks, and Generative AI systems that create text, code, images, or structured outputs. The candidate should also explain that not every AI feature needs an LLM; classification, forecasting, anomaly detection, and ranking may be better solved with traditional ML.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for AI Fundamentals?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise ai fundamentals setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: Compare supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and reinforcement learning.
questions.scenario: You need to select an approach for fraud labels, customer segmentation, and an agent optimizing a workflow.
questions.expectedAnswer: Supervised learning uses labeled examples to learn input-output mappings. Unsupervised learning discovers patterns without labels, such as clusters or lower-dimensional structure. Reinforcement learning trains an agent to choose actions that maximize reward through interaction with an environment. A good answer includes where each approach fits and why data availability drives the choice.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.

questions.title: What is the difference between training and inference?
questions.scenario: Your team trained a model offline and now needs to expose it through an API.
questions.expectedAnswer: Training is the offline or iterative process of learning model parameters from data. Inference is using the trained model to make predictions or generate outputs for new inputs. Production inference emphasizes latency, throughput, cost, reliability, monitoring, and safety, while training emphasizes data quality, optimization, validation, and experimentation.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.

questions.title: How do you explain overfitting and underfitting?
questions.scenario: A model has excellent training accuracy but poor validation accuracy.
questions.expectedAnswer: Overfitting happens when a model learns noise or overly specific training patterns and fails to generalize. Underfitting happens when the model is too simple, poorly trained, or missing important features. Candidates should discuss train-validation gaps, regularization, validation design, leakage detection, and remedies such as more data, better features, early stopping, or simpler models.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.

questions.title: How do you decide whether to build or buy an AI solution?
questions.scenario: Leadership asks whether to buy a vendor AI assistant or build an internal enterprise assistant.
questions.expectedAnswer: The decision depends on strategic differentiation, data sensitivity, integration complexity, time to market, cost, compliance, quality requirements, vendor lock-in, and internal operating maturity. Buy when the use case is commodity and speed matters. Build when the data, workflow, model behavior, or governance requirements create durable differentiation.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.

questions.title: How would you explain bias-variance tradeoff in a AI Fundamentals interview?
questions.scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through bias-variance tradeoff for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should define bias-variance tradeoff, explain why it matters for ai fundamentals, and connect it to a practical implementation decision. The candidate should describe the core mechanism, identify where it appears in a production system, discuss tradeoffs such as quality, cost, latency, safety, governance, and maintainability, and name how they would evaluate whether the approach is working. For senior or architect-level interviews, the answer should also include failure modes, ownership boundaries, and rollout considerations.
